question: A Raleigh applicant asks what entry-level real estate license status North Carolina uses. The issue is raised by Jordan Ellis during agency disclosure review.
choices: North Carolina issues salesperson licenses only
North Carolina issues appraiser licenses through NCREC
North Carolina allows unlicensed assistants to independently list property
North Carolina issues broker licenses; most new individual licensees start as provisional brokers
explanation: North Carolina is a broker-only licensing state. Most new individual licensees receive an inactive broker license on provisional status and become active only when properly affiliated and supervised.

question: A buyer agent wants to qualify for the North Carolina broker license examination through the standard education path. The issue is raised by Taylor Reyes during offer preparation.
choices: Complete only continuing education after passing the exam
Complete no education if sponsored by a firm
Complete the 75-hour Broker Prelicensing Course unless the Commission grants an allowed education/experience qualification
Complete a 30-hour salesperson course
explanation: The standard education path is the North Carolina Broker Prelicensing Course containing at least 75 instructional hours. Other qualification options exist, but sponsorship by a firm does not erase the education requirement.

question: A Asheville candidate asks for the core individual license qualifications in North Carolina. The issue is raised by Morgan Shah during due diligence negotiation.
choices: Pass only the national section regardless of application basis
Be at least 18, have a Social Security number, satisfy lawful-presence/work authorization rules, pass required exam sections, and satisfy character review
Be 21 and hold a college degree in any subject
Have only a firm endorsement and a local business license
explanation: NCREC lists age, Social Security number, lawful presence/work authorization, education qualification, application filing, required exam passage, and character fitness as core individual broker license qualifications.

question: A property manager holds an active real estate license in another state and asks how North Carolina treats the examination requirement. The issue is raised by Riley Chen during trust-account review.
choices: Current licensees from another jurisdiction may qualify to take only the NC state section or bypass the exam under the Commission rules
Every out-of-state licensee must retake both sections
Every out-of-state licensee is automatically active in NC
Only the national section is ever required for out-of-state applicants
explanation: Under the current licensure option, an applicant licensed in another jurisdiction may be allowed to take only the NC state section or bypass the exam, depending on the option used and Commission requirements.

question: A Greensboro applicant discloses a prior criminal matter on the license application. The issue is raised by Cameron Brooks during closing coordination.
choices: Character review occurs only after the first renewal
Only traffic citations are reviewed
A broker-in-charge can waive all character requirements
A criminal record report and character disclosure review may be required before license issuance
explanation: NCREC requires applicants to satisfy the Commission that they possess the requisite character for licensure. Criminal record reports and disclosure documents may be part of that review before issuance.

question: A limited nonresident commercial broker begins discussing confidential needs with a prospective buyer for a office building. The issue is raised by Logan Foster during closing coordination.
choices: Wait until closing to explain agency relationships
Use only a verbal explanation after the offer is accepted
Provide the brochure only to sellers, never buyers
Provide and review the Working With Real Estate Agents disclosure at first substantial contact
explanation: North Carolina brokers use the Working With Real Estate Agents disclosure to explain agency choices at first substantial contact, before the consumer shares confidential information.

question: A buyer working with a Boone broker is ready to submit an offer under buyer agency. The issue is raised by Micah Bell during BIC file review.
choices: A written agreement is required only after loan approval
A buyer agency agreement is never required in NC
Reduce the buyer agency agreement to writing no later than submission of an offer
Buyer agency may remain oral through closing
explanation: NC practice allows an oral buyer agency relationship for a limited time, but it must be put in writing no later than the time an offer is submitted on behalf of the buyer.

question: A New Bern owner wants a broker to list a historic home for sale. The issue is raised by Jamie Ortiz during post-closing complaint intake.
choices: Only the buyer agency agreement must ever be written
A listing agreement must be written from the beginning of the seller agency relationship
A listing can stay oral until the property goes under contract
A listing agreement is optional if the MLS is not used
explanation: Seller agency through a listing agreement must be in writing. Unlike short-lived oral buyer agency, a listing is not properly created as an open-ended oral agreement.

question: One Charlotte firm may represent both the buyer and seller in the same transaction. The issue is raised by Robin Carter during first substantial contact.
choices: Dual agency requires informed written authority from the parties before the broker may act as a dual agent
Dual agency is automatic whenever two brokers share a firm
Dual agency eliminates all loyalty duties
Dual agency can be created secretly after closing
explanation: North Carolina permits dual agency only with proper informed authorization. The firm must handle confidentiality carefully and cannot treat dual agency as automatic or undisclosed.

question: A broker-in-charge asks how one firm can represent both parties while assigning separate agents. The issue is raised by Alex Grant during agency disclosure review.
choices: Designated agency means the agents work for separate firms
Designated agency is always prohibited in NC
Designated agency lets confidential information be shared freely across sides
Designated agency allows the firm to appoint different agents for each side when properly authorized
explanation: Designated agency is a form of authorized dual agency in which the firm designates different agents to work with each party. It requires proper authorization and protection of confidential information.

question: A Durham broker helps a buyer but is acting as a seller subagent in the MLS offer of cooperation. The issue is raised by Bailey Hughes during offer preparation.
choices: A seller subagent has no duties to anyone
A seller subagent automatically becomes a dual agent
A seller subagent owes agency duties to the seller, not to the buyer
A seller subagent represents the buyer
explanation: A seller subagent works on behalf of the seller and owes agency duties to the seller, while still owing honesty and fairness to the buyer. This is why agency disclosure is tested heavily in NC.

question: A seller tells a listing agent privately that the seller will accept much less because of job relocation. The issue is raised by Dakota Ward during due diligence negotiation.
choices: Confidential information can be shared with any buyer who asks
The broker must preserve confidential information such as motivation, bargaining limits, and personal financial pressure
The broker should disclose every client motive to speed negotiations
Confidential information expires when an offer is rejected
explanation: Agency duties include confidentiality. A broker must not disclose a client’s private motivation or bargaining position unless authorized or legally required.

question: A Wilmington listing broker speaks with an unrepresented buyer at a showing. The issue is raised by Elliot Stone during trust-account review.
choices: A broker must treat all parties honestly even when representing only one party
A broker may mislead customers if the client benefits
Honesty is owed only to clients
Nonclients have no protection under NC brokerage rules
explanation: A broker owes loyalty to the client but must still deal honestly and fairly with customers. Agency status does not permit misrepresentation.

question: A broker knows of a serious structural issue affecting a commercial lease. The issue is raised by Kris Bennett during closing coordination.
choices: Only facts written on the seller disclosure form matter
Material facts may be hidden if the client instructs silence
Only facts discovered by the buyer inspector matter
Material facts known or reasonably discoverable by the broker must be disclosed
explanation: North Carolina brokers must disclose material facts they know or should reasonably know. Client loyalty does not authorize concealment of material facts.

choices: Agency status matters only after closing
Consumers must guess who the broker represents
The broker should clarify agency status before receiving confidential information
The broker should collect confidential details first and explain agency later

question: A buyer representative receives a buyer request to be shown only neighborhoods with a certain protected-class makeup. The issue is raised by Casey Alvarez during BIC file review.
choices: Fair housing laws apply only to lenders
Brokers may steer buyers based on neighborhood demographics
Protected-class questions can be answered with discriminatory recommendations
Fair housing laws prohibit discrimination in covered housing based on protected characteristics
explanation: Federal and state fair housing principles prohibit discriminatory conduct such as steering. Brokers should focus on objective property criteria and avoid protected-class recommendations.

question: A buyer wants to use a historic home for short-term rentals, but private restrictions may prohibit it. The issue is raised by Quinn Patel during post-closing complaint intake.
choices: Only public zoning can matter
Covenants disappear at each resale
An owner’s association or restrictive covenant issue can be a material fact if it affects the property or buyer’s intended use
Private restrictions are never material
explanation: Private restrictions and association rules can materially affect a buyer’s intended use. A broker should identify known issues and advise review of governing documents.

question: A seller completes the NC residential property disclosure form for a townhome listing. The issue is raised by Drew Lawson during agency disclosure review.
choices: The form lets sellers hide known defects
The Residential Property and Owners’ Association Disclosure Statement is a seller disclosure tool, but brokers still have independent material fact duties
The disclosure form eliminates all broker disclosure duties
The form is required only for commercial leases
explanation: The seller disclosure form is important in NC residential sales, but it does not eliminate the broker’s independent duty to disclose known or reasonably discoverable material facts.

question: A Durham buyer asks whether severed mineral rights could affect a residential purchase. The issue is raised by Emerson Reed during offer preparation.
choices: Mineral, oil, and gas rights disclosure may be required in covered residential transactions
Mineral rights are never relevant in NC
Only the lender must disclose mineral rights
Mineral rights disclosure applies only after closing
explanation: North Carolina has specific mineral, oil, and gas rights disclosure requirements in covered residential transactions. Brokers should recognize the issue and ensure proper forms are addressed.
